text: The topic of autonomous agents is not new to either the academic or the commercial world. Most current implementations train their agents in some simulator environment, using an assortment of learned and hand-coded knowledge, which are almost always based on non-human, non-intuitive ways of making decisions and hence do not lead to truly human-level decision making behavior. With the more recent advances in large language models (LLMs) trained on web-scale amounts of data, the interest in developing LLM-based autonomous agents has surged and we provide here a systematic and in-depth review of this increasingly growing area of research. We first outline a new unifying approach for constructing such agents while embedding existing works into a new unified architecture. Then, we give an overview of current applications of agent-based research spanning from the social and natural sciences as well as engineering. We follow that by discussing approaches to measure and assess the performance and intelligence of these agents. Finally, based on current challenges and following a reflection of the recent advances in the field, we highlight the key research avenues that need to be addressed in this exciting area.
explanation: The passage is well-structured and uses generic, textbook-style language typical of AI-generated abstracts; it lacks concrete details or citations that often appear in human-authored reviews.
